topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was leaving. The subject line was terse, professional, utterly devoid of any warmth. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of the library suddenly seeming too harsh, too revealing. My hands, resting on the worn cover of my annotated manuscript, began to tremble. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a cold, nauseating sense of betrayal. I reread the closing remarks, searching for some hidden justification, a shred of respect that would make this sudden departure palatable. There was nothing but boilerplate pleasantries. I slowly closed my laptop, the click of the lid echoing loudly in the otherwise silent study carrel. A tightness constricted my chest, making each breath shallow and painful. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding like a declaration of war. I needed air, needed distance from the sterile air of that office, from the ghost of the approval I had so desperately craved.

[story 2]

"He's gone," Sarah murmured, her voice flat, as I watched Professor Davies pack his desk. The air in the faculty lounge felt suddenly thin, charged with an unspoken finality. I felt a profound internal churning, a physical reaction that settled low in my stomach, making me want to physically recoil. I watched him meticulously organize his papers, a practiced, almost ritualistic movement that felt utterly hollow now. I didn't bother to offer a greeting. My gaze remained fixed on the stack of unbound drafts, the tangible evidence of months spent under his exacting, suffocating scrutiny. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I stood up abruptly, the movement jerky, and walked toward the exit. Every step felt weighted, as if I were wading through thick, cold mud. I kept my eyes down, refusing to meet anyone's gaze, acutely aware of the scrutiny I felt directed at me—the student whose project was now adrift, whose lifeline had been severed.

[story 3]

I found myself pacing the length of my dorm room, the cheap carpet muffling the sound of my frantic footsteps. The news had been delivered via a hurried conversation in the hallway, and the information had curdled instantly in my gut. It wasn't anger that surfaced, not precisely; it was a deep, visceral revulsion at the sheer, casual abandonment of responsibility. I ran a hand over my face, feeling the clammy heat rise on my skin. Why? Why this sudden, unceremonious exit? It felt like a deliberate erasure, as if the entire intellectual scaffolding I had built over the last year was being swept away by a careless hand. I slammed my textbook shut, the sound sharp and brittle. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I sank onto the edge of my bed, my breathing ragged, feeling utterly hollowed out, like a vessel that had just been emptied of its essential contents.
